**Wusong**, [formerly romanized](/wiki/Postal_romanization "Postal romanization") as **Woosung**,[[n 1]](#cite_note-2) is a [subdistrict](/wiki/Subdistrict "Subdistrict") of [Baoshan](/wiki/Baoshan_District,_Shanghai "Baoshan District, Shanghai") in northern [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). Prior to the city's expansion, it was a separate port town located 14 miles (23 km) down the [Huangpu River](/wiki/Huangpu_River "Huangpu River") from Shanghai's urban core.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wusong&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Wusong is named for the Wusong River, a former name for Shanghai's [Suzhou Creek](/wiki/Suzhou_Creek "Suzhou Creek"). Suzhou Creek is now a tributary to the [Huangpu River](/wiki/Huangpu_River "Huangpu River"), emptying into it in [Puxi](/wiki/Puxi "Puxi") across from [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui "Lujiazui") and just north of the [Bund](/wiki/The_Bund "The Bund"). The Huangpu had previously been a tributary to the Wusong, but the two reversed their importance when a flood caused it to gain a number of the Wusong's former tributaries. The location where the Huangpu and Wusong meet was generally known as *Wusongkou* ("mouth of the Wusong"). As a result of an American railroader visiting the area in his sea captain days, it would also become the namesake for an unincorporated community in southwestern Ogle County, Illinois, northwest of Dixon.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wusong&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Wusong housed a [Qing](/wiki/Qing_Empire "Qing Empire") fortress protecting the entrance to Shanghai.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESladen1895[httpsarchiveorgstreamjapsathome00sladuoftpage278mode2up_278]-1) It was captured by the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") during the [Battle of Woosung](/wiki/Battle_of_Woosung "Battle of Woosung") on 16 June 1842, amid the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"). During the steamship era, it was the point of departure for large [steamers](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship") bound for Shanghai.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESladen1895[httpsarchiveorgstreamjapsathome00sladuoftpage278mode2up_278]-1) This position caused it to be the site of China's first telegraph wires and [first railroad](/wiki/Woosung_Road "Woosung Road"), both running to Shanghai along what is today the route of the [Shanghai Metro](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro "Shanghai Metro")'s elevated [Line 3](/wiki/Line_3,_Shanghai_Metro "Line 3, Shanghai Metro").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By 1900, it boasted a [lighthouse](/wiki/List_of_lighthouses_in_China "List of lighthouses in China") and a "skeleton" [teahouse](/wiki/Teahouse "Teahouse"), as well as a small squadron of war-[junks](/wiki/Junk_(ship) "Junk (ship)") (*ty-mung*) of the [Imperial Chinese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Chinese_Navy "Imperial Chinese Navy").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESladen1895[httpsarchiveorgstreamjapsathome00sladuoftpage278mode2up_278]-1) [Tongji University](/wiki/Tongji_University "Tongji University") was founded here in 1909.

In the opinion of some historians, the [Battle of Shanghai](/wiki/Battle_of_Shanghai "Battle of Shanghai") represented the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in Asia[[2]](#cite_note-3) and Wusongkou was the scene of an all-out land, sea and air battle, as Imperial Japanese Marines landed here on 23 August 1937, and were attacked by [Chinese Air Force](/wiki/Republic_of_China_Air_Force "Republic of China Air Force") [Hawk III](/wiki/Curtiss_BF2C "Curtiss BF2C") fighter-attack planes escorted by [P-26/281 Peashooters](/wiki/Boeing_P-26_Peashooter "Boeing P-26 Peashooter"); the intense dogfight between the Chinese fighters and [IJN fighters](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy_Air_Service "Imperial Japanese Navy Air Service") from aircraft carriers *[Hōshō](/wiki/Japanese_aircraft_carrier_H%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D "Japanese aircraft carrier Hōshō")* and *[Ryūjō](/wiki/Japanese_aircraft_carrier_Ry%C5%ABj%C5%8D "Japanese aircraft carrier Ryūjō")* resulted in several Chinese fighters shot down, while the Japanese lost two [A4N](/wiki/Nakajima_A4N "Nakajima A4N") fighters, each claimed by Capt. [Liu Cuigang](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%88%98%E7%B2%B9%E5%88%9A "cmn:刘粹刚") and Lt. [John Huang](/wiki/John_Huang_Xinrui "John Huang Xinrui"), although Capt. Liu's victim managed to nurse his crippled A4N back to *Ryūjō*.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) Wusong was later the site of an internment camp for marines captured on [Wake Island](/wiki/Wake_Island "Wake Island") after the [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") over four years later.

Wusong became a district of Shanghai, before it was abolished in 1988 and incorporated into [Baoshan District](/wiki/Baoshan_District,_Shanghai "Baoshan District, Shanghai").[[6]](#cite_note-toponymy-7)

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wusong&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

The Wusung Radio Tower is a 321-metre-tall [guyed mast](/wiki/Guyed_mast "Guyed mast") situated at Wusong near [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). The Wusung Radio Mast was built in the 1930s and was at the time of inauguration the world's second-tallest architectural structure after the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building").[[7]](#cite_note-8)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wusong&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Variant spellings include **Woo-Sung**.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESladen1895[httpsarchiveorgstreamjapsathome00sladuoftpage278mode2up_278]-1)
